era_context: Early Dynastic Period in Mesopotamia (c. 3150–2334 BCE); emergence of the first city-states in southern Mesopotamia (Sumer). Egypt in the Early Dynastic Period (c. 3100–2686 BCE), shortly after the unification of Upper and Lower Egypt under the First Dynasty. The Indus Valley Civilization is in its early phases. Writing systems are nascent or recently developed in Mesopotamia and Egypt.

events.title: Early Dynastic Period consolidation in Mesopotamian city-states
events.description: During this period, Sumerian city-states such as Uruk, Lagash, Umma, and Ur were consolidating political and religious authority. Archaeological evidence shows increasing monumental architecture, temple complexes, and administrative systems. The exact events of 3110 BCE are not individually documented, but this year falls within the broader Early Dynastic I phase when city-states were establishing themselves as the dominant political units in southern Mesopotamia.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Uruk, Lagash, and other Sumerian sites; stratigraphy and pottery chronology
Historiographic consensus on Early Dynastic chronology

events.certainty_note: No specific events are recorded for this exact year. Dating of the Early Dynastic Period itself remains debated; this year is assigned within the conventional chronology, but absolute dating in this era is uncertain by ±50–100 years.

events.title: Early Egyptian First Dynasty rule
events.description: Egypt's First Dynasty (c. 3100–2890 BCE) was in its early phase. Pharaohs such as Narmer (or Menes in later tradition) and his successors were consolidating control over unified Upper and Lower Egypt. Monumental tombs (mastabas) were being constructed at Abydos and Saqqara. The exact events of 3110 BCE are not individually attested, but this year falls within the period of early dynastic consolidation.
key_figures: Narmer/Menes (traditional founder of unified Egypt, though dating is disputed)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from royal tombs at Abydos and Saqqara
Manetho's Aegyptiaca (3rd century BCE compilation of earlier king lists)

events.certainty_note: The First Dynasty is well-attested archaeologically, but the precise dating and identification of individual pharaohs remain contested. The conventional date for the start of the First Dynasty (c. 3100 BCE) is itself debated; some scholars place it as late as 3000 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional dating of the Early Dynastic Period and the First Dynasty of Egypt is not universally accepted. Some scholars argue for a 'low chronology' that would place these events 100–150 years later. The identification of Narmer as the unifier of Egypt is also debated; some scholars see the unification as a more gradual process. No specific events are attested for 3110 BCE itself, making this year essentially a placeholder within a broader era.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 3110 BCE is almost entirely archaeological. No contemporary written records survive from this year or even this decade. The chronology is based on stratigraphic sequences, pottery typology, and later king lists compiled centuries or millennia after the events. Egyptian chronology in this period is particularly uncertain due to gaps in the archaeological record and debates over the length of individual reigns. Mesopotamian chronology is similarly uncertain. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite contexts (temples, tombs, palaces) and tell us little about the broader population.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Early Dynastic consolidation in Mesopotamia
Continuation of First Dynasty rule in Egypt
